title: QA: glob cache get-then-insert race in PermissionManager::glob_to_regex_cached
description:
## Severity
Minor
## Dimension
concurrency
## Location
src/permissions.rs:327-350
## Violation
The cache lookup-then-insert is split across two lock acquisitions with
a `drop(cache)` in between:
```rust
let cache = GLOB_CACHE.lock().unwrap_or_else(...);
if let Some(re) = cache.get(pattern) { return Some(re.clone()); }
let regex_str = Self::glob_to_regex(pattern);
let result = Regex::new(&regex_str);
drop(cache);
match result {
    Ok(re) => {
        GLOB_CACHE.lock()...
            .insert(pattern.to_string(), re.clone());
```
Two threads racing on the same uncached pattern both compile the regex
twice (waste) and one's `insert` overwrites the other. Functionally
correct (the two compiled regexes are equivalent), but the pattern is
brittle: any future change that makes regex compilation observably
different (e.g. id'd regex objects, metrics, allocator pressure) will
make this a real bug. Plus `unwrap_or_else(PoisonError::into_inner)`
silently continues with a poisoned cache rather than rebuilding.
## Mandated refactor
Use `HashMap::entry(pattern.to_string()).or_insert_with(|| ...)` under
a single lock — that holds the lock during compilation but eliminates
the race and the double-compile. Or switch to `OnceLock<Regex>` keyed by
pattern via `dashmap` / `once_cell` if hold-during-compile is a
performance concern. Replace the silent poison-recovery with an
explicit `expect("GLOB_CACHE poisoned")` so corruption surfaces.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
